ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 22, 38, 653024, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 22, 38, 653033, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 22, 38, 653389, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f13-c3bb-7585-8f9d-0f613610fd87', conversation_id='019f5f13-c3bb-7585-8f9d-0f6080593ee9')

Let me review the target specs again:

1. **Account**: 
   - Id from source account.id
   - Name from account.name (trimmed/initcased)
   - ERP_Number__c: NULL (no source column maps to this)
   - Customer_Tier__c: from account.tier (map Gold/Silver/Bronze/Platinum)
   - Region__c: from account.region
   - Industry: from account.industry
   - Website, BillingCity, BillingCountry: no source data → NULL
   - Legacy_Customer_ID__c: from account.id
   - CreatedDate/LastModifiedDate: CURRENT_DATE or similar
   - IsDeleted: 0

2. **Contact**:
   - Id: from contact.id
   - FirstName/LastName: split from contact.full_name
   - Email: from contact.email
   - Phone, Title, Preferred_Language__c: no source data → NULL (but Role is needed)
   - Role__c: enum [Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor] - need to derive somehow
   - AccountId: need to join/contact.account_ref with account.id - but they're both "ACC-XXXX" format, so direct mapping works
   - Legacy_Contact_ID__c: from contact.id
   - CreatedDate/LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted
   
The source contact data shows id like "CON-00001", account_ref like "ACC-1001" or NULL, and full_name as a space-separated string. For AccountId, I'll use the account_ref directly since it matches the account.id format in the target.

For Role__c with limited source data (just company_name), I'll default to "End User" when account_ref exists but has no specific role indicators, falling back to NULL otherwise. Since there are no explicit timestamp columns in the source, I'll use CURRENT_DATE for created and modified dates.

Looking at opportunity.sql, I see a major issue: it's using a correlated subquery that references the physical table instead of the dbt source macro, which will break when dbt materializes the model. The AccountId logic needs to reference {{ source(...) }} properly. For CloseDate with no direct source mapping, I'll use NULL or a default date string. The currency handling and Amount column look correct as-is since the amounts are already numeric doubles.

I also notice some potential naming inconsistencies between account.id values and opportunity.account_name - there are formatting variations like spacing around ampersands that could cause lookup failures. A direct join on matching names might be unreliable, so I should consider using customer_number to account_id mapping instead. Let me focus on fixing the most critical issues first: correcting the source macro usage, ensuring proper case handling, and removing references to physical tables in favor of dbt sources.

For the role determination logic, if account_ref is present it indicates a relationship to an account, otherwise I'll default to NULL or a standard placeholder value. Now I'll start rewriting these models.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='Let me fix the issues with contact.sql and opportunity.sql. The main problems are:
1. `contact.sql` has malformed CASE syntax for Role__c and uses raw table references instead of dbt sources
2. `opportunity.sql` references a physical table in a subquery instead of using dbt source macros
3. Several date/legacy column issues across models'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/contact.sql", "content": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\n\nSELECT\n    c.id AS \"Id\",\n    CASE WHEN ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), 1) = 1 THEN ''\n         ELSE SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1)\n    END AS \"FirstName\",\n    CASE WHEN ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), 1) = 1 THEN c.full_name\n         ELSE TRIM(ARRAY_TO_STRING(ARRAY(SELECT x FROM UNNEST(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1)) AS x LIMIT (ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1)) - 1)), ' '))\n    END AS \"LastName\",\n    c.email AS \"Email\",\n    NULL::TEXT AS \"Phone\",\n    NULL::TEXT AS \"Title\",\n    CASE WHEN c.account_ref IS NOT NULL THEN 'End User' ELSE NULL END AS \"Role__c\",\n    NULL::TEXT AS \"Preferred_Language__c\",\n    a.id AS \"AccountId\",\n    c.id AS \"Legacy_Contact_ID__c\",\n    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'contact') }} c\nLEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} a\n    ON c.account_ref IS NOT NULL AND a.id = c.account_ref\n"}', tool_call_id='call_4a3ffi3j')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=19192, output_tokens=1115), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 33, 57, 841111, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 33, 57, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-881ac6fe-228a-4034-99d7-9d963f1c7456', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 22, 38, 653024, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 22, 38, 653033, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 22, 38, 653389, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f13-c3bb-7585-8f9d-0f613610fd87', conversation_id='019f5f13-c3bb-7585-8f9d-0f6080593ee9')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Looking at the written files, I see several issues that need to be fixed:

1. **contact.sql** has a corrupted CASE expression for `Role__c`:
```sql
CASE INITCAP(c.company_name)
    WHEN 'Unknown' THEN NULL
    ELSE INITCAP(c.account_ref) IS NOT NULL AND INITCAP(c.account_ref) <> '' THEN 'Decision Maker'
    ELSE 'End User'
END AS "Role__c",
```
This is invalid SQL. The CASE expression structure is wrong.

2. **contact.sql** also has issues:
   - It references `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact` directly instead of using `{{ source(...) }}`
   - `AccountId` uses a subquery that references the raw table, not a dbt source macro
   - Date columns are NULL without proper handling

3. **opportunity.sql** has issues:

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720496, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  function pg_catalog.ltrim(integer, unknown) does not exist
  LINE 36: ...WHEN s.customer_number IS NOT NULL THEN 'ACC-' || TRIM(LEADI...
                                                                ^
  HINT:  No function matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql
- contact: Database Error in model contact (models/contact.sql)
  function array_length(text, integer) does not exist
  LINE 16:     CASE WHEN ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...
                         ^
  HINT:  No function matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql", timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720504, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720781, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebd74df3b70', conversation_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebc1e9d8470')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content="The errors indicate two issues:

1. In `opportunity.sql`: `ltrim(integer, unknown)` does not exist - this means `TRIM(LEADING ...)` is being called on an integer (likely a concatenated expression where one part is an integer). I need to cast the customer_number or the entire concatenated string properly.

2. In `contact.sql`: `array_length(text, integer)` does not exist - `ARRAY_LENGTH` in PostgreSQL takes only 1 argument (the array), not 2. It should be `ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...)` — wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me look more carefully. The error says `ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...)`. 

Actually, `SPLIT_PART` returns text, not an array. And `ARRAY_LENGTH` takes only one argument (an array). So the code seems to be trying to use `ARRAY_LENGTH` incorrectly - maybe it should just check the length of a string? Or perhaps it was trying to do something like check if there are N parts.

Let me read both files to understand the current state and fix them.", id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content="I'll start by reading both failing models to understand their current code, then fix them."), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_gk4w2cgi'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/contact.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_1yd20u73')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=1729, output_tokens=323), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 48, 252084, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 48, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-6f62fbf7-e81b-4129-8790-1e7fff9aedfd', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content="Now I can see the issues:

1. **opportunity.sql**: The error is `ltrim(integer, unknown) does not exist`. Looking at line 36 (in compiled code), the problematic expression is:
   ```sql
   'ACC-' || TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM CAST(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4) AS INTEGER))
   ```
   
   The issue is that `TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM <integer>)` - TRIM operates on strings, not integers. After casting to INTEGER, the result of `SUBSTRING(...)` cast to INTEGER is being passed to TRIM which expects a string. I need to first remove leading zeros from the string, then cast to integer.
   
   Fix: Cast SUBSTRING result to TEXT first (it already is text), or use LTRIM on the text before casting:
   ```sql
   'ACC-' || CAST(LTRIM(CAST(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4) AS TEXT), '0') AS INTEGER)
   ```
   Or better, since SUBSTRING returns text:
   ```sql
   'ACC-' || CAST(LTRIM(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4), '0') AS INTEGER)
   ```

2. **contact.sql**: The error is `array_length(text, integer) does not exist`. `ARRAY_LENGTH` in PostgreSQL takes only ONE argument - the array itself. It returns the total number of elements. `SPLIT_PART` returns a text string, not an array.

   Looking at what the code is trying to do:
   - `FirstName`: Get the first word from full_name, but if there's only one word, use empty string ''
   - `LastName`: If there's only one word, use the whole name; otherwise get everything after the first word
   
   The fix for FirstName: Check if there are spaces in the name instead of trying to use ARRAY_LENGTH. We can check if SPLIT_PART returns something different from the original, or count spaces.
   
   A simpler approach: Check if the full_name contains a space.
   
   For FirstName: `CASE WHEN position(' ' IN c.full_name) > 0 THEN split_part(c.full_name, ' ', 1) ELSE '' END`
   
   For LastName: If no space, use full_name; otherwise, get everything after first space.
   ```sql
   CASE WHEN position(' ' IN c.full_name) = 0 THEN c.full_name
        ELSE TRIM(SUBSTRING(c.full_name FROM position(' ', c.full_name) + 1))
   END
   ```

   Let me rewrite both models properly.", id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='I can see the two issues now. Let me fix them:

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

## Transformation rules

- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

   ```sql
   'ACC-' || TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM CAST(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4) AS INTEGER))
   ```
   
   The issue is that `TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM <integer>)` - TRIM operates on strings, not integers. After casting to INTEGER, the result of `SUBSTRING(...)` cast to INTEGER is being passed to TRIM which expects a string. I need to first remove leading zeros from the string, then cast to integer.
   
   Fix: Cast SUBSTRING result to TEXT first (it already is text), or use LTRIM on the text before casting:
   ```sql
   'ACC-' || CAST(LTRIM(CAST(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4) AS TEXT), '0') AS INTEGER)
   ```
   Or better, since SUBSTRING returns text:
   ```sql
   'ACC-' || CAST(LTRIM(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4), '0') AS INTEGER)
   ```

2. **contact.sql**: The error is `array_length(text, integer) does not exist`. `ARRAY_LENGTH` in PostgreSQL takes only ONE argument - the array itself. It returns the total number of elements. `SPLIT_PART` returns a text string, not an array.

   Looking at what the code is trying to do:
   - `FirstName`: Get the first word from full_name, but if there's only one word, use empty string ''
   - `LastName`: If there's only one word, use the whole name; otherwise get everything after the first word
   
   The fix for FirstName: Check if there are spaces in the name instead of trying to use ARRAY_LENGTH. We can check if SPLIT_PART returns something different from the original, or count spaces.
   
   A simpler approach: Check if the full_name contains a space.
   
   For FirstName: `CASE WHEN position(' ' IN c.full_name) > 0 THEN split_part(c.full_name, ' ', 1) ELSE '' END`
   
   For LastName: If no space, use full_name; otherwise, get everything after first space.
   ```sql
   CASE WHEN position(' ' IN c.full_name) = 0 THEN c.full_name
        ELSE TRIM(SUBSTRING(c.full_name FROM position(' ', c.full_name) + 1))
   END
   ```

   Let me rewrite both models properly.", id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='I can see the two issues now. Let me fix them:

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720496, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  function pg_catalog.ltrim(integer, unknown) does not exist
  LINE 36: ...WHEN s.customer_number IS NOT NULL THEN 'ACC-' || TRIM(LEADI...
                                                                ^
  HINT:  No function matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql
- contact: Database Error in model contact (models/contact.sql)
  function array_length(text, integer) does not exist
  LINE 16:     CASE WHEN ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...
                         ^
  HINT:  No function matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql", timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720504, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720781, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebd74df3b70', conversation_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebc1e9d8470')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content="The errors indicate two issues:

1. In `opportunity.sql`: `ltrim(integer, unknown)` does not exist - this means `TRIM(LEADING ...)` is being called on an integer (likely a concatenated expression where one part is an integer). I need to cast the customer_number or the entire concatenated string properly.

2. In `contact.sql`: `array_length(text, integer)` does not exist - `ARRAY_LENGTH` in PostgreSQL takes only 1 argument (the array), not 2. It should be `ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...)` — wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me look more carefully. The error says `ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...)`. 

Actually, `SPLIT_PART` returns text, not an array. And `ARRAY_LENGTH` takes only one argument (an array). So the code seems to be trying to use `ARRAY_LENGTH` incorrectly - maybe it should just check the length of a string? Or perhaps it was trying to do something like check if there are N parts.

Let me read both files to understand the current state and fix them.", id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content="I'll start by reading both failing models to understand their current code, then fix them."), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_gk4w2cgi'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/contact.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_1yd20u73')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=1729, output_tokens=323), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 48, 252084, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 48, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-6f62fbf7-e81b-4129-8790-1e7fff9aedfd', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebd74df3b70', conversation_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebc1e9d8470')

    CAST(s.name AS TEXT) AS "Name",

        WHEN 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN 'proposal/price quote' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'

        WHEN 'closed lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate: source is not directly available, need to derive or use NULL
    NULL AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(s.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION) AS "Amount",
    'EUR' AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    -- AccountId: map customer_number (KD-XXXX) to account Id (ACC-XXXX)
    CASE WHEN s.customer_number IS NOT NULL THEN 'ACC-' || TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM CAST(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4) AS INTEGER)) ELSE NULL END AS "AccountId",
    -- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c
    CAST(s.id AS TEXT) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    -- CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate: not in source, use placeholder or NULL
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} s

    END AS "LastName",

    c.id AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",

LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} a

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720496, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

  function pg_catalog.ltrim(integer, unknown) does not exist

                                                                ^
  HINT:  No function matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql
- contact: Database Error in model contact (models/contact.sql)
  function array_length(text, integer) does not exist
  LINE 16:     CASE WHEN ARRAY_LENGTH(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1), ...
                         ^
  HINT:  No function matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql", timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720504, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 34, 31, 720781, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebd74df3b70', conversation_id='019f5f1e-a527-735d-9089-6ebc1e9d8470')

   The issue is that `TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM <integer>)` - TRIM operates on strings, not integers. After casting to INTEGER, the result of `SUBSTRING(...)` cast to INTEGER is being passed to TRIM which expects a string. I need to first remove leading zeros from the string, then cast to integer.

   ```sql
   'ACC-' || CAST(LTRIM(CAST(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4) AS TEXT), '0') AS INTEGER)
   ```
   Or better, since SUBSTRING returns text:
   ```sql
   'ACC-' || CAST(LTRIM(SUBSTRING(s.customer_number FROM 4), '0') AS INTEGER)
   ```

2. **contact.sql**: The error is `array_length(text, integer) does not exist`. `ARRAY_LENGTH` in PostgreSQL takes only ONE argument - the array itself. It returns the total number of elements. `SPLIT_PART` returns a text string, not an array.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272336, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

                                                                ^
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272344, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272650, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f20-7057-76e8-873d-3cafefeed57e', conversation_id='019f5f20-7057-76e8-873d-3cae93568d3c')

- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272336, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272336, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272344, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 5, 36, 29, 272650, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5f20-7057-76e8-873d-3cafefeed57e', conversation_id='019f5f20-7057-76e8-873d-3cae93568d3c')
